title: Air-dry clay herb markers
subtitle: Hand-stamped clay tags for identifying pots and beds
excerpt: Small oval or teardrop clay tags stamped with herb names and a pointed stake attached at the base. Made in air-dry clay with no kiln. Sealed with two coats of exterior PVA to withstand some outdoor use, though they fare best under cover.
sourceNotes: Impressed lettering in clay is a practice as old as writing. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness and cut to shape.
Unfired clay in any drying stage before it is fired or fully set.

content.text: Clay herb markers are pressed from a rolled
slab

, cut to shape, and pressed with letters or stamps before drying. A pointed clay stake scores and slips to the base of each tag. No kiln is required. The markers are best used in covered beds or windowsill pots; long outdoor exposure in wet weather will eventually soften unglazed air-dry clay even when sealed.
Steps
Roll the clay to 6 mm thick and cut oval or teardrop tag shapes.
Press letter stamps into the soft clay to mark each herb name.
Score and slip a wire stake to the base of each tag.
Dry flat on a board to bone-dry, then sand and seal with varnish.
Rolling and cutting

Roll the clay to 6 mm thick using slab guides or two even sticks. Cut oval or teardrop tag shapes approximately 60 mm x 40 mm with the fettling knife. Roll thin triangular stakes 80 mm long and 8 mm across at the base, tapering to a point. Make one stake per tag.
Stamping the herb names

While the tags are still soft, press individual letter stamps firmly into the clay surface. Work from the centre outward and align letters by eye or mark a light guide line with the needle tool first. If writing freehand, incise letters with the needle tool, pressing 2 mm deep. Common herbs to mark: Basil, Thyme, Sage, Rosemary, Mint, Parsley, Chives, Coriander. Keep the lettering simple.
Attaching the stakes

Score and slip the wide base of each stake and the matching bottom edge of the tag. Press the stake flat against the back of the tag, seam pointing upward, so the tag forms the visible face and the stake points downward from behind. Reinforce the join with a thin clay collar smoothed around the joint. Allow to stiffen for 20 minutes before drying.
Drying and sealing
Lay the markers flat on the board to dry.
Greenware

air-dry clay is fragile; handle gently until fully dry. Once dry (two days), paint with acrylic paint if desired, then apply three coats of exterior-grade PVA or acrylic varnish, paying attention to all edges and the stake. The stake especially needs thorough sealing to slow moisture absorption when pushed into compost.
Variations
Use paper clay for the stake; the cellulose fibres make it more resistant to cracking when pushed into compost.
Cut the tags as leaf shapes and impress a leaf vein pattern with the needle tool before adding the herb name.
Make larger 100 mm x 70 mm versions for outdoor raised beds painted with exterior acrylic paint and three coats of yacht varnish.

items.symptom: Stake snaps at the join during drying
items.cause: Join was not scored and slipped, or stake was too thin
items.fix: Make the stake base at least 12 mm wide. Score deeply and use thick slurry

items.cause: Clay was too soft when stamping; the edges collapsed
items.fix: Let the tag stiffen on the board for ten minutes before stamping. Press stamps straight down and lift straight up without rocking

items.cause: Insufficient sealing, especially on edges and stake
items.fix: Apply three coats of exterior-grade PVA and pay extra attention to end grain at cut edges
